mc_a3.name: Treating A2L like propane (A3)
mc_a3.desc: You are mapping "flammable" straight to "explosive". A2L is its own class: high ignition energy, slow flame speed — a different risk profile from A3 hydrocarbons.
mc_a3.fix: A2L = lower toxicity + LOWER flammability: burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s and hard to ignite. Respect ignition-source rules, but do not carry A3 assumptions into A2L work.
mc_a3.ref: ASHRAE 34 safety classes; ESCO "A2L Refrigerant Safety" course
mc_a3.plan: Re-read the ASHRAE 34 class matrix (A/B × 1/2L/2/3) until you can reproduce it from memory.

mc_class.name: ASHRAE 34 letter-number system is fuzzy
mc_class.desc: The safety-class code is a two-axis system and you are blending the axes: the letter is toxicity, the number is flammability.
mc_class.fix: A = lower toxicity, B = higher toxicity; 1 = no flame propagation, 2L = lower flammability (≤10 cm/s), 2 = flammable, 3 = higher flammability. R-32 and R-454B are both A2L.
mc_class.plan: Make a 2×4 flash card of the ASHRAE 34 matrix with one example refrigerant per cell.

mc_retrofit.name: Thinking A2L is a drop-in retrofit
mc_retrofit.desc: You are treating the R-410A → A2L change like R-22 → R-407C. It is not a retrofit path: A2L safety design (sensors, airflow, ignition control) is built into listed new equipment.
mc_retrofit.fix: Never charge an A2L refrigerant into a system listed for R-410A — it violates the UL 60335-2-40 listing, codes, and manufacturer instructions. A2L refrigerants go only into equipment designed and listed for them.
mc_retrofit.ref: UL 60335-2-40; OEM bulletins (Carrier/Daikin) on R-454B & R-32
mc_retrofit.plan: Memorize the one-liner: "A2L is a platform change, not a retrofit."

mc_service_ban.name: Overcorrecting: "R-410A is now illegal"
mc_service_ban.desc: You have over-applied the 2025/2026 transition: the bans target NEW equipment, not the installed base.
mc_service_ban.fix: Existing R-410A systems may be serviced and repaired indefinitely — with virgin (while supplies last), recovered or reclaimed R-410A. What ended is installing new R-410A systems, not servicing old ones.
mc_service_ban.ref: EPA Technology Transitions FAQ; AIM Act phasedown schedule
mc_service_ban.plan: Learn the transition as two lists: what is banned (new equipment) vs what continues (service, reclaim).

mc_tools.name: A2L tool compatibility is guesswork
mc_tools.desc: Either "all my R-410A tools are fine" or "I must re-buy everything" — both cost you: one is a safety/compliance risk, the other wastes money.
mc_tools.fix: Must be A2L-rated: recovery machine (spark-free) and electronic leak detector (UL/EN listed for A2L). Generally carry over: gauges, hoses (check ratings), scales, and most vacuum pumps — verify manufacturer listing.
mc_tools.ref: AHRI Safe Refrigerant Transition Task Force guidance; tool OEM A2L compatibility lists
mc_tools.plan: Audit your kit against an OEM A2L-compatibility list and tag each tool OK / replace.

mc_props.name: Guessing A2L pressures & behavior instead of using data
mc_props.desc: You are extrapolating from R-410A habits instead of the actual property data of the new refrigerants.
mc_props.fix: R-454B runs close to R-410A with slightly LOWER pressures and a small (~1.5°F) glide; R-32 runs similar-to-slightly-higher pressures with notably hotter discharge. Always use the PT chart for the actual refrigerant.
mc_props.ref: OEM PT charts; this site's Refrigerant Properties tool (R-32 / R-454B included)
mc_props.plan: Pull up R-32 and R-454B on the refrigerant-properties tool and compare saturation pressures with R-410A at 45°F / 105°F.

mc_gwp.name: Confusing "lower GWP" with "no GWP / no rules"
mc_gwp.desc: Low-GWP marketing language has slid into "environmentally free pass" in your model of the rules.
mc_gwp.fix: R-454B GWP ≈ 466 and R-32 ≈ 675 — roughly 78% / 68% below R-410A (≈2088), but far from zero. Venting and recovery rules apply to A2Ls exactly as to other regulated refrigerants.
mc_gwp.ref: AR4/AR5 GWP tables; EPA venting prohibition (CAA §608(c))
mc_gwp.plan: Memorize three GWP anchors: R-410A ≈ 2088, R-32 ≈ 675, R-454B ≈ 466.

mc_charge.name: Blend-charging rules half-remembered
mc_charge.desc: Fractionation logic is inverted in your recall: blends separate in the VAPOR phase, so you avoid that by moving liquid.
mc_charge.fix: Charge zeotropic/near-azeotropic blends (R-454B, like R-410A before it) as LIQUID from the cylinder, metering into the suction side as needed. Single-component R-32 has no glide and can be charged in either phase and topped off freely.
mc_charge.plan: Write the rule once: "Blends leave the tank as liquid; singles don't care."

mc_vent.name: Venting legality reasoned from ODP/GWP instead of the rule
mc_vent.desc: You are deciding what may be released based on "does it harm ozone / climate" logic. The law does not work that way — it names what is prohibited and what is exempt.
mc_vent.fix: Knowingly venting ANY regulated refrigerant or substitute (CFC, HCFC, HFC, HFO/A2L blends) is illegal — currently up to $124,426 per day per violation. Exemptions are specific and listed by EPA (e.g., de minimis releases during good-faith recovery, nitrogen holding charges, and certain exempted substitutes like CO₂). "Low GWP" is not an exemption.
mc_vent.ref: 40 CFR 82.154; EPA Section 608 venting prohibition fact sheet
mc_vent.plan: Memorize the short exemption list — everything not on it stays in the system or the cylinder.

mc_evac.name: Recovery evacuation requirements not solid
mc_evac.desc: Two separate vacuums are blending together: the EPA recovery table (inches Hg, depends on appliance size/class) and the deep-vacuum dehydration target (microns) used before recharging.
mc_evac.fix: Recovery: EPA table sets inches-Hg targets by appliance class/size and recovery-machine vintage (e.g., R-22 ≥200 lbs → 10 in Hg with a post-1993 machine). Dehydration/evacuation before charging: 500 microns is industry practice, not an EPA recovery number.
mc_evac.ref: 40 CFR 82.156 required-evacuation tables; EPA 608 Type II prep material
mc_evac.plan: Print the EPA evacuation table and quiz yourself until the R-22 rows are automatic.

mc_rrr.name: Recover / recycle / reclaim blurred
mc_rrr.desc: The three R's differ by WHERE the refrigerant can go next — that is the part your definitions are missing.
mc_rrr.fix: Recover = remove and store, no processing. Recycle = on-site cleanup (oil separation, filter-driers), reuse in same/same-owner equipment. Reclaim = reprocess to AHRI 700 purity with chemical analysis — required before refrigerant changes ownership; done by EPA-certified reclaimers.
mc_rrr.plan: Anchor each R to its destination: same system / same owner / open market.

mc_cyl.name: Cylinder handling by feel, not by rule
mc_cyl.desc: Fill limits and cylinder rules exist to keep hydrostatic pressure from turning a tank into a projectile — they are exam favorites precisely because people wing them.
mc_cyl.fix: Recovery cylinders: fill to max 80% of capacity, use DOT-approved recovery cylinders (yellow top / gray body), never mix refrigerants in one tank, and transport upright & secured.
mc_cyl.ref: DOT 49 CFR; EPA 608 Core cylinder-safety section
mc_cyl.plan: Remember "80 / DOT / don't mix / upright" as one phrase.

mc_hfc_scope.name: Stuck in the pre-2026 "608 = ozone only" world
mc_hfc_scope.desc: Your mental model of Section 608 stops at CFC/HCFC. Since the AIM Act and the 2024 Refrigerant Management (ERR) rule, HFCs and their substitutes are inside the management regime.
mc_hfc_scope.fix: From January 1, 2026, leak-repair and related refrigerant-management requirements apply to appliances with ≥15 lbs of HFC or HFC-substitute refrigerant (GWP > 53) — and 608 certification is required to handle A2Ls like R-32/R-454B.
mc_hfc_scope.ref: EPA 2024 ERR (Emissions Reduction & Reclamation) rule; AIM Act subsections
mc_hfc_scope.plan: Update your 608 notes: scope now includes HFCs/A2Ls — mark every "ODS-only" statement as outdated.

mc_thresholds.name: Leak-rate trigger numbers shuffled
mc_thresholds.desc: The three trigger rates are easy to transpose under exam pressure — and one of them almost certainly appears on your test.
mc_thresholds.fix: Annualized leak-rate triggers: 10% comfort cooling · 20% commercial refrigeration · 30% industrial process refrigeration. (Applies to appliances above the charge threshold — ≥15 lbs for HFCs under the 2026 ERR provisions.)

mc_dates.name: Running on last year's transition headlines
mc_dates.desc: The transition rules MOVED in 2026 and your recall is a snapshot of the old coverage: the famous "no R-410A installs after Jan 1 2026" deadline was first bridged by enforcement discretion, then permanently removed by EPA's May 2026 reconsideration rule.
mc_dates.fix: What stands: since Jan 1 2025, no manufacture/import of new residential & light-commercial AC/HP products using refrigerants with GWP ≥ 700 (e.g., R-410A). What changed: the Jan 1 2026 installation deadline is gone — systems built from pre-2025 components may be installed until supplies run out (VRF has its own later dates).
mc_dates.ref: EPA Technology Transitions reconsideration final rule (May 26, 2026; effective July 27, 2026)
mc_dates.plan: Update your notes: 2025 = "no new R-410A boxes built" still true; the install deadline was REMOVED in May 2026 — track the rule, not the headline.

mc_a2l_cert.name: Inventing a separate "EPA A2L license"
mc_a2l_cert.desc: Manufacturers, distributors and some AHJs require A2L training — your brain has upgraded that into a new federal certification that does not exist.
mc_a2l_cert.fix: EPA Section 608 remains THE federal certification for handling regulated refrigerants, including A2Ls. A2L safety training (ESCO, OEM, ACCA) is strongly expected — and often required by employers/suppliers — but it is not a separate EPA card.
mc_a2l_cert.ref: EPA 608 program pages; ESCO A2L training program description
mc_a2l_cert.plan: Keep the two layers separate in your notes: federal 608 certification vs industry A2L training.

mc_type1_scope.name: Stretching Type I past small appliances
mc_type1_scope.desc: You are letting "small charge" stand in for "small appliance" — Type I is defined by the equipment category, not just by how many pounds are in it.
mc_type1_scope.fix: A small appliance is a pre-assembled, hermetically sealed unit factory-charged with ≤5 lbs of refrigerant (fridges, window units, small reach-ins, PTACs, water coolers). A split system with a small charge is still NOT a small appliance — it needs Type II.
mc_type1_scope.ref: EPA 608 Core/Type I definitions; 40 CFR 82 Subpart F
mc_type1_scope.plan: Test yourself: "factory-sealed AND ≤5 lb AND not a split system" — all three, or it is not Type I.

mc_openbook.name: Assuming any section can be taken open-book
mc_openbook.desc: You generalized one exception into a blanket rule for the whole certification.
mc_openbook.fix: Type I is the ONLY Section 608 section that may be administered open-book. Type II, Type III and the Core exam (and therefore Universal) must be closed-book.
mc_openbook.ref: EPA 608 certifying-organization program rules (ESCO, Mainstream Engineering, etc.)
mc_openbook.plan: One-liner: "Type I is the exception, everything else is closed-book."

mc_lowpressure.name: Mixing up low-pressure vs high-pressure system logic
mc_lowpressure.desc: You are applying high-pressure-system intuition (leaks push refrigerant OUT) to a low-pressure system, where the physics runs the other way.
mc_lowpressure.fix: Type III low-pressure appliances (chillers using low-pressure refrigerants) run BELOW atmospheric pressure through most of their cycle. A leak there pulls air and moisture IN rather than pushing refrigerant out — which is why these systems need a purge unit and a different troubleshooting mindset.
mc_lowpressure.ref: EPA 608 Type III material; chiller OEM manuals
mc_lowpressure.plan: Flip the mental model for Type III: "sub-atmospheric — air leaks IN, not refrigerant OUT."

mc_purge.name: Misreading what a purge unit actually does
mc_purge.desc: The name "purge" is pulling your answer toward "removes refrigerant" — it does the opposite job.
mc_purge.fix: A purge unit on a low-pressure chiller removes the non-condensable air/moisture that leaks INTO the sub-atmospheric system, venting that air (not refrigerant) while recovering any refrigerant vapor that came along with it. It is not a refrigerant-recovery device for the main charge.
mc_purge.ref: Low-pressure chiller OEM/service literature; EPA 608 Type III
mc_purge.plan: Anchor: "purge unit = air/moisture out, refrigerant stays in."

mc_confined_space.name: Skipping the confined-space threshold test
mc_confined_space.desc: You are deciding "enough combustion air" by eyeballing the room instead of running the actual volume-per-input check the code uses.
mc_confined_space.fix: A widely used mechanical/fuel-gas-code test: a space is "confined" when it provides less than roughly 50 cubic feet of volume per 1,000 Btu/h of the COMBINED input rating of all fuel-burning appliances in it. Confined spaces need a dedicated combustion-air path; large "unconfined" spaces may already have enough air by volume/infiltration alone.
mc_confined_space.ref: Widely adopted mechanical/fuel-gas code combustion-air provisions (IMC-style; also NFPA 54)
mc_confined_space.plan: Practice the math once: add up appliance Btu/h, divide room volume by (total Btu/h ÷ 1000), compare to 50.

mc_combustion_source.name: Assuming indoor air is always an acceptable combustion-air source
mc_combustion_source.desc: You are treating the indoor-air method as the default everywhere, when it is only valid when the space itself is large enough (or specifically connected) to supply the air.
mc_combustion_source.fix: Codes offer two conceptual paths: the INDOOR air method (relying on the space's own volume/openings to adjacent spaces — workable mainly in unconfined spaces) and the OUTDOOR air method (dedicated openings or ducts bringing air from outside — required once a space is confined). A confined mechanical room almost always needs the outdoor-air path.
mc_combustion_source.ref: Mechanical/fuel-gas code combustion-air methods (indoor vs outdoor air)
mc_combustion_source.plan: Ask "is this space confined?" first — that answer picks the method, not preference.

mc_duct_material.name: Treating all duct materials/locations as interchangeable
mc_duct_material.desc: You are picking duct material by what is on the truck rather than by where the duct runs and what it passes through.
mc_duct_material.fix: Ducts routed through rated assemblies, plenums, or certain occupancy types must use listed/labeled materials meeting specific flame-spread and smoke-developed limits (or be protected by fire/smoke dampers) — not just "any" galvanized or flexible duct.
mc_duct_material.ref: Mechanical code duct-construction/fire-safety provisions (IMC-style, Ch. 6 concept area)
mc_duct_material.plan: Before running a duct, ask what it passes through (rated wall? plenum? return path for a specific occupancy?) — that answer picks the allowed material.

mc_duct_clearance.name: Ignoring clearance-to-combustibles for ducts near heat sources
mc_duct_clearance.desc: You are assuming "it is just sheet metal" makes a duct immune to the same clearance logic that applies to the appliance it is connected to.
mc_duct_clearance.fix: Ducts routed near or connected to heat-producing equipment still need the same kind of clearance to combustible materials (or approved thermal protection) that the appliance itself requires — this is a common inspection citation on retrofit jobs.
mc_duct_clearance.plan: Treat "clearance to combustibles" as following the heat source through the whole duct run, not stopping at the appliance cabinet.

mc_vent_category.name: Flattening vent categories into "old vs new equipment"
mc_vent_category.desc: You are using equipment age as a proxy for the actual two-factor definition the code uses.
mc_vent_category.fix: Modern gas-appliance vent categories (I–IV) are defined by TWO factors: vent static pressure (negative/natural-draft vs positive/fan-assisted) and flue-gas temperature (non-condensing vs condensing). A furnace is not "Category IV" just because it is new — it is Category IV because it is positive-pressure AND condensing.
mc_vent_category.plan: Draw the 2×2 grid once (pressure × condensing) and place one example appliance in each cell.

mc_b_vent.name: Over-extending what a Type B vent is listed for
mc_b_vent.desc: Because Type B vent is common and familiar, you are assuming it is a universal solution rather than a listed product with real limits.
mc_b_vent.fix: Type B (double-wall metal) vent is listed for certain natural-draft, Category I gas appliances only. It is NOT rated for oil-fired or solid-fuel appliances, and it is not what most high-efficiency condensing (Category IV) furnaces vent through — those use special listed vent piping per the manufacturer's instructions.
mc_b_vent.ref: Vent/chimney listing standards; appliance manufacturer venting instructions
mc_b_vent.plan: Before specifying Type B, confirm: gas-fired, natural-draft, Category I — all three.

mc_shared_vent.name: Assuming any appliances can share a vent if the math works
mc_shared_vent.desc: You are treating vent sizing as a pure capacity/math problem and skipping the compatibility question entirely.
mc_shared_vent.fix: Connecting a positive-pressure, condensing (Category IV) appliance to the same flue as a natural-draft (Category I) appliance is a classic, dangerous violation — the pressurized/condensing appliance can force combustion gases backward into the other appliance's draft, or its condensate can damage a flue never designed for it. Category compatibility comes before sizing math.
mc_shared_vent.plan: Rule of thumb: never let a fan-assisted condensing appliance share a flue with a natural-draft one, no matter what the size tables say.

mc_machineryroom_scope.name: Assuming every A2L install needs a mechanical-room detection system
mc_machineryroom_scope.desc: You are applying commercial-equipment-room rules to a small residential split system that never needed them.
mc_machineryroom_scope.fix: Most residential A2L split systems stay within ASHRAE 15.2 charge limits and rely on the INDOOR UNIT'S OWN integral leak-detection/mitigation built in per its UL 60335-2-40 listing — not a separate mechanical-room ventilation and detection system. Dedicated machinery-room ventilation/detection becomes a code question mainly for larger charges, commercial equipment rooms, or indoor installations that exceed listed charge limits.
mc_machineryroom_scope.ref: ASHRAE 15.2 charge limits; UL 60335-2-40 integral safety systems; mechanical code machinery-room provisions (Ch. 11 concept area)
mc_machineryroom_scope.plan: Ask "charge size relative to ASHRAE 15.2 limit, and is this a dedicated equipment room?" before assuming a separate detection system is required.

mc_sensor_place.name: Placing refrigerant sensors like a natural-gas detector
mc_sensor_place.desc: You are reflexively mounting the detector high because that is the habit for lighter-than-air natural gas leaks.
mc_sensor_place.fix: Most common A2L refrigerants (R-32, R-454B) are HEAVIER than air. Where a dedicated detection system is used, sensors are typically mounted LOW (near floor level), the opposite placement from a natural-gas detector.
mc_sensor_place.ref: A2L refrigerant vapor-density data; equipment installation/listing instructions
mc_sensor_place.plan: Two-line rule: natural gas → lighter than air → detector high. A2L refrigerant → heavier than air → detector low.

mc_ventilation_rate.name: Ventilation-rate logic is fuzzy (purpose, method, and table structure)
mc_ventilation_rate.desc: You are blending ventilation air with comfort conditioning, filtration, or duct sizing — related systems, but governed by separate code logic.
mc_ventilation_rate.fix: Minimum outdoor-air ventilation rates exist to dilute indoor contaminants/CO2 (an indoor-air-quality purpose, distinct from comfort heating/cooling capacity). Codes typically allow natural ventilation (sized operable openings) as an alternative to mechanical ventilation in eligible occupancies. Any major exhaust system (like a kitchen hood) raises a separate makeup-air/pressure-balance question. Ventilation-rate tables usually combine a per-person term with a per-area term, varying by occupancy type.
mc_ventilation_rate.ref: Mechanical code ventilation provisions (IMC-style Ch. 4 concept area; ASHRAE 62.1 referenced logic)
mc_ventilation_rate.plan: Separate four ideas cleanly: ventilation purpose (IAQ dilution) vs comfort conditioning vs filtration vs makeup air — each is a distinct code concern.
